7: David’s promise to Mephibosheth — lōʾ tîrāʾ kî ʿāśōh ʾeʿeśeh ʿimmᵉḵā ḥeseḏ biʿăḇûr yᵉhônātān ʾāḇîḵā, ‘do not fear, for I will surely show you kindness for the sake of your father Jonathan’ — is one of the most typologically dense scenes in the Davidic narrative. Three elements converge: (1) ḥeseḏ (covenant-loyalty) extended to the one who has no claim on it, (2) inheritance restored (wᵉhašiḇōtî lᵉḵā ʾet kol śᵉdēh šāʾûl), and (3) permanent table-fellowship (tāmîd, ‘always’). Mephibosheth arrives crippled in both feet (v3), summoned from Lo-debar (‘no pasture’ — the place of nothing), with every reason to fear political execution as a rival claimant. What he receives instead is ḥeseḏ grounded in a prior covenant relationship he did not himself make. This is the formal structure of the gospel: those brought to the Messianic table are brought on the basis of a covenant made between another and the King, not on their own merit or wholeness. Luke 14:21 consciously echoes this scene: ‘go out quickly to the streets and lanes of the city and bring in the poor and crippled and blind and lame (ptōchous kai anapeirous kai typhlous kai chōlous).’ Luke 22:30 is the eschatological tāmîd: ‘that you may eat and drink at my table in my kingdom.’ Ephesians 2:12-13 supplies the theological structure: those who were ‘strangers to the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world’ are brought near ‘by the blood of Christ’ — the ḥeseḏ of a covenant extended across the gap of unworthiness.
13: The narrator’s closing statement of the Mephibosheth episode — wayyēšeḇ mᵉpîḇōšet bîrûšālaim kî ʿal šulḥan hammelek tāmîd hûʾ ʾōḵēl wᵉhûʾ pissēaḥ šᵉtê raglāyw, ‘so Mephibosheth lived in Jerusalem, for he ate at the king’s table always; now he was lame in both feet’ — juxtaposes permanent table-honor and permanent lameness without resolving the tension. His lameness is not healed; it is noted alongside his station at the royal table. This juxtaposition is the typological key: adoption precedes transformation. Mephibosheth does not receive wholeness as the condition of his welcome; he receives ḥeseḏ while still broken, and the narrative preserves the brokenness as a permanent marker of his identity at the table. Verse 11b’s statement that David treated him ‘like one of the king’s sons’ (kᵉʾaḥaḏ mibbanê hammelek) applies the adoption category: not a servant, not a tolerated guest, but a son. Galatians 4:5-7 is the NT formal equivalent: ‘so that we might receive adoption as sons... so you are no longer a slave, but a son, and if a son, then an heir through God.’ John 1:12: ‘to all who did receive him... he gave the right to become children of God.’ The crippled man at the king’s table, treated as a son, permanently resident in Jerusalem — this is the eschatological picture of the church as the adopted household of the Messianic king, still marked by the fall’s effects, already welcomed to the table that will be set ‘always.’
1: David's opening question — 'Is there still anyone left of the house of Saul that I may show him kindness (ḥeseḏ) for Jonathan's sake?' — frames the entire chapter as an act of covenant faithfulness. Ḥeseḏ is the great covenant-love word of the OT, the loyalty that goes beyond what duty strictly requires. David acts not because Mephibosheth deserves restoration but because of his covenant with Jonathan (1 Samuel 18:3; 2 Samuel 20:15-17; 2 Samuel 23:18). The type is unmistakable: God's grace to the sinner is not grounded in the sinner's merit but in his covenant with the Son — 'for Jesus' sake.' The question 'Is there still anyone?' echoes the seeking Shepherd (Luke 15:4) and the gracious Father watching for the prodigal (Luke 15:20).
2: Ziba, a servant of Saul's house, is summoned — a man who has survived the transition of power by serving whoever holds it. David learns from Ziba that Jonathan's son remains, establishing the investigative pattern of grace: the king seeks out the object of covenant-kindness before that person can seek him. The antitype is the divine initiative in salvation: God does not wait for sinners to find him but seeks them (Luke 19:10; John 6:44; Romans 5:8). Ziba's role as intermediary who brings information about the hidden beneficiary also typifies the witness function — like John the Baptist announcing the lost sheep that the Shepherd will find.
3: David repeats his covenant-question with an added qualifier: 'that I may show him the kindness of God (ḥeseḏ ʾĕlōhîm).' The phrase escalates the covenant-love to divine proportion — this is not merely political courtesy but godlike mercy. The addition of ʾĕlōhîm points the typology explicitly upward: David's act toward Mephibosheth is a parable of what God does toward sinners. Paul uses the exact same logic in Romans 5:6-8 — while we were still helpless, God demonstrated his love; and in Ephesians 2:1-9 the spiritually 'crippled' are raised to sit at the King's table (Ephesians 2:6) not by their own merit but by the grace of the covenant.
4: Mephibosheth is identified as being at the house of Machir the son of Ammiel in Lo-debar. Lō-dĕḇār means 'no pasture' or 'nothing' — a place of deprivation, the condition of a refugee from a fallen dynasty hiding in obscurity. The place-name functions typologically: the sinner before grace is in the land of nothing, without pasture, without belonging. Amos 6:13 uses Lo-debar as an ironic reference (Israel taking 'nothing' as a military trophy), deepening the spiritual resonance. David's messengers going to Lo-debar enacts what the gospel does — it enters the land of spiritual nothingness and calls out the one who has no claim to the king's attention.
5: David sends and fetches Mephibosheth from Lo-debar — the king's initiative traversing the distance between the palace and the refugee's hiding place. The verb wayyišlaḥ (he sent) and wayyiqqāḥēhû (he took/brought him) are the two movements of grace: the sending of the invitation and the drawing of the person to the king. In John's Gospel the same two movements characterize the Incarnation: 'God so loved the world that he gave (sent) his Son' (John 3:16) and 'No one comes to me unless the Father who sent me draws him' (John 6:44). Mephibosheth does not travel to David; David's messengers come to him. Grace is prevenient — it goes before.
6: When Mephibosheth comes before David, he falls on his face in obeisance and calls himself David's servant. His prostration and self-deprecation before the king ('your servant') mirror the posture of every sinner encountering sovereign grace — undone by undeserved favor, offering nothing but submission. The parallelism with the prodigal son's confession (Luke 15:21, 'I am no longer worthy to be called your son; make me as one of your hired servants') is precise: both Mephibosheth and the prodigal expect rejection and receive restoration. The reversal is grace's signature: the one who prostrates himself is lifted up (Matthew 23:12; Luke 14:11).
8: Mephibosheth calls himself a 'dead dog' (keleb mēt) — the lowest self-designation in the OT idiom, a creature without standing, without value, without claim. The phrase echoes his grandfather Saul's self-deprecation to David (1 Samuel 24:14) but in a context of pure helplessness rather than false humility: Mephibosheth genuinely has nothing to offer. The phrase activates the Psalm 22 register where the sufferer is surrounded by dogs (Psalms 22:16) and the greater Son of David is treated as a dead dog by his enemies. But here the logic reverses: the dead dog is not abandoned but adopted. Christ, who in his passion was treated as a dead dog (Isaiah 53:3), raises the spiritually dead to life (John 11:25-26).
9: David calls Ziba and transfers to Mephibosheth all the land of Saul and his house — a massive economic restoration of the inheritance Mephibosheth had lost. This is not merely welfare but full covenant reinstatement: the land is Mephibosheth's by right of Jonathan's covenant, now formally restored by royal decree. The legal structure typifies justification: not an improvement of status but a full declaratory restoration of what was lost by the covenant representative. Adam lost the inheritance; the Second Adam (Christ) restores it to all who are in him by faith (Romans 5:17-19; Ephesians 1:11). The very land Saul had held against David is now the gift David gives to Saul's heir.
10: Ziba, his fifteen sons and twenty servants are commanded to farm the land for Mephibosheth, providing the covenant-beneficiary with the means to live without labor in the land. The abundance of provision — fifteen sons and twenty servants mobilized for one man — illustrates the overflowing surplus of grace. This excess points toward the Messianic promise of Amos 9:13-14 (wine dripping from the mountains, plowman overtaking the reaper) and the abundance of the new creation where there will be no poverty or scarcity. Mephibosheth is set up as a permanent beneficiary of covenant kindness: ḥeseḏ does not give once but establishes an ongoing structure of care.
11: Ziba acknowledges the king's command, and the narrator adds the programmatic summary: Mephibosheth shall eat at David's table like one of the king's sons. 'Like one of the king's sons' is the Johannine 'children of God' language (John 1:12): the adopted stranger eats with the family as if born to it. In the NT this reality is described as adoption (huiothesia, Romans 8:15; Ephesians 1:5) — the Spirit of sonship who cries 'Abba, Father' makes the formerly alien sinner a co-heir with Christ (Romans 8:17). Mephibosheth did not become a son of David in fact; he ate as one by covenant grace. Believers are not sons of God by nature but are constituted sons by the Spirit through faith in the Son.
12: Mephibosheth has a young son named Mika — the covenant-kindness to Jonathan extends to a third generation, establishing a dynasty of grace that continues after Mephibosheth. This multi-generational reach of ḥeseḏ corresponds to the covenant promise that extends 'to a thousand generations of those who love me and keep my commandments' (Deuteronomy 7:9; Exodus 20:6). Grace is not a momentary provision but a living covenant that propagates through time. Christologically, the Son of David's grace similarly creates a new family that extends to every generation and nation (Psalms 22:30-31; Revelation 7:9) — those grafted in by faith produce heirs of the covenant who also come to the King's table.
